mapping.a_element: single ion reaction events as a probe for branching ratios and decay probabilities
mapping.b_element: single atoms in optical traps as a probe for quantum state control and precision metrology

mapping.a_element: Mg+ ion in a specific excited state (3p 2P3/2) interacting with molecular hydrogen isotopes
mapping.b_element: neutral atoms in specific electronic states experiencing state-insensitive trapping potentials

mapping.a_element: branching ratio between MgD+ and MgH+ formation from HD reactions
mapping.b_element: coherent control of electronic transitions independent of atomic motion

mapping.a_element: quantitative information from few reaction events (small sample size)
mapping.b_element: precision measurements from single-atom or few-atom systems

brief_justification: While both papers use single-particle systems (ions or atoms) to extract precise information, paper A focuses on chemical reaction dynamics and isotope effects via branching ratios and decay probabilities, whereas paper B centers on quantum state engineering and metrology using state-insensitive optical traps. The underlying mechanisms—chemical reaction kinetics vs. quantum optical control—are fundamentally distinct.
